intro: JavaScript's dynamic, single-threaded runtime gives way to Java's strongly typed, multithreaded JVM when converting in this direction. Every variable and return type needs an explicit type declaration, `Promise`-based async patterns become Java's `CompletableFuture` or synchronous blocking calls, and JavaScript's objects become Java classes with defined fields and constructors. Java's verbosity is offset by compile-time correctness guarantees.

diffs.from: Dynamic. Types resolved at runtime. No compile step.
diffs.to: Static, nominal typing. Every variable and method needs a declared type.

diffs.from: Single-threaded event loop. Promises and async/await are idiomatic.
diffs.to: Multi-threaded. CompletableFuture for async; ExecutorService for thread pools.

diffs.from: Prototype chain. Functions are objects. No interface keyword.
diffs.to: Nominal class hierarchy. interface, abstract class, extends, implements.
Packages and imports. Build with Maven or Gradle. No native bundler.

diffs.from: null + undefined, optional chaining (?.), nullish coalescing (??).
diffs.to: null only. NullPointerException. Use Optional<T> for explicit absence.

examples.title: Async function → CompletableFuture
examples.description: JS Promises translate to Java CompletableFuture with method chaining.

examples.title: Plain object → Java class
examples.description: A JavaScript object literal with methods becomes a fully typed Java class.

tips.title: Add type declarations everywhere
tips.body: Every variable, parameter, and return type needs a type. Start by inferring types from usage, then declare String, int, boolean, List<T>, etc.

tips.title: Promise → CompletableFuture
tips.body: async functions return CompletableFuture<T>. .then() maps to .thenApply(). .catch() maps to .exceptionally(). Promise.all becomes CompletableFuture.allOf().

tips.title: Wrap optionals in Optional<T>
tips.body: JS functions returning null/undefined should return Optional<T> in Java. Callers then explicitly call .orElse() or .orElseThrow() rather than null-checking.

tips.title: Object literals become classes
tips.body: Every { key: value } object needs a corresponding class or record in Java. Records (Java 16+) are the most concise: record User(String name, int age) {}.
